---
title: "Sequence Alignment"
subtitle: "Lecture 01"
format:
  revealjs:
    # NOTE: theme paths are resolved relative to THIS document, unlike
    # `{{< include >}}` paths, which are resolved from the project root.
    theme: [default, ../../styles/slides.scss]
    slide-number: true
    # Live annotation during the lecture. NOTE: chalkboard is incompatible with
    # `embed-resources`, so slides cannot be made self-contained while this is
    # on. Slides are therefore delivered via the hosted site (Moodle links to
    # it), not as a standalone file. If you ever need a single-file deck, either
    # drop chalkboard or export to PDF with `?print-pdf` in the browser.
    chalkboard: true
    incremental: false
    code-line-numbers: true
    width: 1280
    height: 720
---

## Learning objectives

By the end of this session you should be able to:

- explain why sequence similarity implies possible homology
- compute a global alignment by hand with Needleman–Wunsch
- distinguish global from local alignment and choose appropriately
- read a scoring matrix and justify a gap penalty

## Why align sequences?

Two sequences that look similar may share ancestry.

Similarity is *observed*. Homology is *inferred*.

::: {.fragment}
The whole field rests on making that inference carefully.
:::

## Scoring an alignment

{{< include /shared/partials/scoring-scheme.qmd >}}

## Needleman–Wunsch {.smaller}

Fill a matrix $F$ where $F_{i,j}$ is the best score aligning the first $i$ and
first $j$ characters:

$$
F_{i,j} = \max
\begin{cases}
F_{i-1,j-1} + s(x_i, y_j) & \text{(match/mismatch)} \\
F_{i-1,j} + d & \text{(gap in } y) \\
F_{i,j-1} + d & \text{(gap in } x)
\end{cases}
$$

::: {.fragment}
Then trace back from $F_{n,m}$ to recover the alignment itself.
:::

## In code

```{.python code-line-numbers="|1-2|4-6|8"}
from Bio import Align
aligner = Align.PairwiseAligner()

aligner.match_score = 1
aligner.mismatch_score = -1
aligner.open_gap_score = -2

alignments = aligner.align("GATTACA", "GCATGCU")
```

## Global vs. local

| | Global (Needleman–Wunsch) | Local (Smith–Waterman) |
|---|---|---|
| Aligns | full length | best sub-region |
| Use when | sequences comparable in length | domains, motifs, unequal length |
| Negative scores | kept | clamped to zero |

## Next

Practical 01 — implement Needleman–Wunsch yourself, then compare against
Biopython.
